summary: Founder of Stoicism who began teaching at the Stoa Poikile (Painted Porch), giving his school its name. Originally inspired by the Cynics. Associated with the concept of katalepsis (comprehension) and the founding principles of Stoic philosophy.

works.name: Founding of Stoicism and the Stoa Poikile
works.description: Zeno of Citium founded Stoicism after being inspired by the Cynics. He began teaching at a colonnade called the Stoa Poikile (Painted Porch), giving his school its name. This is the most common giveaway identifier for Zeno in quizbowl questions, along with the fact that he was from Citium (in Cyprus).

clues.clue: Founded Stoicism and began teaching at the Stoa Poikile (Painted Porch), giving his school its name
examples: He began teaching at a colonnade called the Stoa Poikile, giving his school its name
Stoicism was founded by this thinker, who was originally inspired by the cynics
this thinker, who was originally inspired by the cynics
Give this first name of that philosopher from Citium who founded Stoicism

works.name: Katalepsis (Comprehension)
works.description: Zeno described a fourfold concept of comprehension (katalepsis) by gradually closing his hand, demonstrating how perception moves from impression to assent to comprehension to knowledge. This concept was central to Stoic epistemology and was attacked by the Academic skeptic Arcesilaus.

clues.clue: Described katalepsis (comprehension) by gradually closing his hand in a fourfold demonstration
examples: One philosopher of this name described a fourfold concept of comprehension by gradually closing his hand; that concept is katalepsis

clues.clue: Arcesilaus attacked his causal theory of perception
examples: One of this author's surviving fragments attacks Zeno of Citium's causal theory of perception

works.name: Dionysius the Renegade and Cyrenaics
works.description: When Dionysius the Renegade asked why Zeno didn't correct his mistakes, Zeno replied 'Because I mistrust you.' Dionysius later rejected Stoicism's forbearance in the face of suffering when his eye hurt and left to study at the Cyrenaics' school, founded by Aristippus.

clues.clue: Told Dionysius the Renegade 'Because I mistrust you' when asked why he didn't correct his mistakes
examples: After Dionysius the Renegade legendarily asked why a philosopher with this first name didn't correct his mistakes, that philosopher replied, 'Because I mistrust you'

clues.clue: Dionysius the Renegade rejected Stoicism's forbearance when his eye hurt and left for the Cyrenaics
examples: Dionysius rejected Stoicism's forbearance in the face of suffering and left to study at this city's philosophical school

comprehensive_summary:
Zeno of Citium was the founder of Stoicism, one of the most influential philosophical schools of the ancient world. Originally from Citium in Cyprus, he was inspired by the Cynics before beginning to teach at the Stoa Poikile (Painted Porch) in Athens, giving his school its name.

Zeno's philosophical contributions include the concept of katalepsis (comprehension), which he demonstrated by gradually closing his hand in a fourfold process showing how perception moves from impression to assent to comprehension to knowledge. This Stoic epistemological concept was attacked by the Academic skeptic Arcesilaus, who challenged Zeno's causal theory of perception.

An anecdote about Zeno involves Dionysius the Renegade, who asked why Zeno didn't correct his mistakes. Zeno replied 'Because I mistrust you.' Dionysius later rejected Stoicism's forbearance in the face of suffering when his eye hurt and left to study with the Cyrenaics, a hedonistic school founded by Aristippus. Dionysius also pranked his other teacher Heraclides Ponticus with a fake play.

Zeno is frequently clued in the context of broader Stoic philosophy, including the Stoic belief in pneuma (breath of life) and apatheia. He shares his first name with Zeno of Elea, the philosopher known for paradoxes about motion (Achilles and the Tortoise, the flying arrow), and questions sometimes disambiguate between the two by accepting either.

cards.front: Philosopher: described a fourfold concept of comprehension (katalepsis) by gradually closing his hand
Philosopher: told Dionysius the Renegade 'Because I mistrust you' when asked why he didn't correct his mistakes
Philosopher: Dionysius the Renegade rejected this thinker's school's forbearance when his eye hurt and left for the Cyrenaics, a hedonistic school founded by Aristippus
Concept: Stoic belief in a substance called the 'breath of life,' composed of fire and air, that structured matter throughout the universe and made up the soul in humans
